semantic_journey: Arabic al-da'arma = short hurried steps, moving in small quick steps. Greek teleodromeo = to complete the course/race (telos+dromos). Short hurried steps (manner of walking) and completing a race (goal achievement) are in different semantic categories despite both involving movement.
key_insight: Short hurried steps (manner of movement) and completing a race (achievement goal) are in different semantic categories despite both involving locomotion.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-kast = an unspecified plant name. Greek lukoskutalion = a plant in the Lycium/boxthorn family. Both are plant names but in different botanical categories. Shared category of plant is not sufficient for cognate status; every culture independently names plants.
key_insight: Two plant names in unrelated botanical categories with no documented etymological connection; shared plant-category is not cognate evidence.

semantic_journey: Arabic asattat al-ardh = the land had no vegetation (barren/still). Greek hestaouta = standing still (perfect participle of histemi = to stand). Barren land being metaphorically still and a person/object literally standing still are different semantic domains. The Arabic encodes absence of growth; Greek encodes a positional state.
key_insight: Barren/ungrowing land and literally standing-still are different semantic domains; the stillness metaphor is too weak a bridge.

semantic_journey: Arabic ghadara = treachery/betrayal; ghadir = shallow pool. Greek Gindaros = Gindarus, a place in the northern Levant. Gindarus is a proper toponym; its Semitic etymology is debated. Arabic ghadara (treachery) or ghadir (pool) does not plausibly name a Syrian fortified site. Proper name exclusion applies.
key_insight: Gindarus is a Levantine proper toponym; assigning it an Arabic betrayal/pool etymology is speculative without documented substrate evidence.

semantic_journey: Arabic khazza = belly-slackening, emptiness of belly (inner looseness implying inner lack/want). Greek khatizo = to be in want, to crave, to lack. Core concept: inner lack. Arabic encodes physical inner looseness (empty belly); Greek encodes experiential want/craving. 1-step drift: empty-belly -> craving/wanting.
key_insight: Arabic belly-emptiness/slackening and Greek craving/wanting share the core concept of inner lack, physical in Arabic and experiential in Greek.

semantic_journey: Arabic gharasa = to plant trees/seeds. Greek Gerasa = Jerash, a major Decapolis city in Jordan (proper name). Even if Gerasa has a Semitic root related to planting, this is substrate toponymy not a cognate of the common verb. Proper name exclusion applies.
key_insight: Gerasa is a proper toponym; even a Semitic planting-root etymology would be substrate toponymy, not a cognate of the verb.

semantic_journey: Arabic sastan = Sistan (a historical Persian/Central Asian region, proper name). Greek Sosthanes = a Greek personal name from Greek roots (sos+sthenos = safe+strength). One is a Persian geographic proper name, one is a Greek personal name. Proper name exclusion applies doubly.
key_insight: A Persian place-name and a Greek personal name from Greek roots are not cognates; proper name exclusion applies to both.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-ghidhy = cutting with an instrument (also nourishment). Greek ligdos = a kind of mortar or hard grinding stone. A cutting instrument and a grinding mortar are both hard tools but operate on different principles (sharp-edge cutting vs circular-pressure grinding). No valid 2-step path.
key_insight: Cutting instruments and grinding mortars are both hard tools but operate on different principles with distinct semantic identities.
